# Hiking Trip Summary — 2026-06-15 through 2026-06-29

**Generated by:** hike-izer Skill (CARD-0073)
**Data window queried:** 2026-06-15T00:00:00Z – 2026-06-29T23:59:59Z
**Data source:** JCTsh Environmental Data Google Sheets (Environmental Data, Hiking Observations, GPS Track), via `action=export`, filtered to `source=hiking-monitor`

> **Corrected 2026-07-18:** the first version of this summary blended in 4,270 rows from `front-porch-temp-sensor` (a separate, stationary home sensor that shares the same Environmental Data sheet) — only 1,932 of the original 6,202 rows were actually from the hiking monitor. Found while testing the Skill against a second date range and adding a `--source` filter to `fetch_hike_data.py` as a result. All numbers below are corrected to hiking-monitor only.

---

## The Trip

This was the hiking-monitor's first extended field deployment — roughly two weeks away from home WiFi, logging continuously in the background while camping. The GPS track shows the device — and presumably its carrier — only really moved twice during the whole window, both on **June 17–18**, high in mountain terrain at roughly 1,312–1,367 meters elevation (4,300–4,485 ft).

**Session 1 — the evening/night leg (June 17, 23:51 UTC → June 18, 02:59 UTC, ~3.1 hours).** Sun position at the start of this stretch was already low — about 28° above the horizon in the west, dropping through the WSW as the session went on — this was a late-afternoon-into-evening push, not a midday hike. By the time it wrapped up just after 3 AM UTC, the sun had long since set; a meaningful chunk of this session happened in darkness.

**Session 2 — the mid-afternoon leg (June 18, 14:46 UTC → 15:55 UTC, ~68 minutes).** Shorter and squarely in daylight hours.

Outside those two windows, the device wasn't silent, but it was quiet — notably, there's a **10-hour gap with no readings at all from June 17, 13:56 UTC through 23:57 UTC**, ending right when Session 1's GPS track picks up. That's consistent with the device being powered down, out of range, or simply packed away for a stretch before that evening's hike — not necessarily a fault, but a much bigger and more real gap than anything visible before the source-filtering fix (which had been papered over by front-porch-temp-sensor's own readings filling the same time slots).

Temperatures ranged from 81.8°F up to a scorching 110.6°F, and humidity from a dry 15.5% up to 48.2% — a real range, but notably narrower than what the original (contaminated) version of this summary reported, since that version's low end was actually front-porch-temp-sensor's cooler overnight-at-home readings, not anything the hiking monitor itself saw in the field.

One thing worth flagging directly: **UV index never rose above 0.1 the entire trip**, despite temperatures hot enough to imply strong sun exposure at points. That's not a plausible "no sun" reading for a two-week outdoor deployment in June — it lines up with the LTR-390 sensor validation concerns already tracked separately (CARD-0065), and is worth treating as a likely sensor/mounting issue rather than an actual low-UV environment.

The battery told its own story. Voltage ranged from a healthy 4.4V down to **2.85V** — a genuinely low reading, consistent with the original LiPo cell failing in the field that's already documented in `README.md` (2026-07-03, replaced from spare stock, firmware low-battery cutoff added afterward). This trip's data is very likely showing that failure unfold in real time.

---

---

## Expected vs. Actual Data Coverage — Pipeline Health Check

This section exists to surface whether the hiking-sensor pipeline itself was working correctly during the trip, not just to describe the hike.

**Environmental Data:** expected ~10,800 readings at the standard 2-minute cadence over the full 15-day query window; **1,932 actual readings from `hiking-monitor` (17.9% coverage)**. Considerably lower than the (wrong) 57.4% first reported — that number was inflated by counting another device's readings. 17.9% is a genuinely low figure for a device meant to log continuously, though some of it is expected: the device wasn't on a continuous hike for 15 straight days, and some stretches were legitimately off/idle (see the 10-hour gap noted above, plus several other multi-minute gaps). Five gaps over 6 minutes were flagged, the largest being that 601.7-minute (10-hour) one on June 17.

**GPS correlation:** of the 1,932 hiking-monitor readings, **1,416 (73.3%) had GPS coordinates successfully matched**, 516 did not. Given only 453 GPS trackpoints exist across two short sessions totaling under 5 hours, and the ±5-minute correlation window, a meaningful chunk of the "missing coordinate" readings are simply outside any window where GPS Track data existed to match against — not a correlation bug, just geometry (see `data-pipeline.md`'s known correlation-gap note).

**GPS Track:** 453 total trackpoints across 2 detected sessions, both with healthy per-session coverage (85.9% and 95.6% of the expected 30-second cadence) — the GPS pipeline itself was working well *during* active use. What's more notable is that only ~4.3 hours of GPS activity were recorded across a ~2-week trip window.

**Hiking Observations: 0 rows.** Either the voice-observation pipeline wasn't yet in regular use during this specific trip, or it simply wasn't used this time — the pipeline itself (Tasker widget → Apps Script → categorized row) was confirmed working in Steps 23–26 testing, so this isn't evidence of a broken pipeline, just an absence of use.

**Bottom line:** once corrected to hiking-monitor's own data, coverage for this trip is genuinely low (17.9%), with a real 10-hour silent stretch on June 17 worth a second look — was the device off, out of range, or just packed away? Combined with the UV sensor issue (CARD-0065) and the zero-observation count, this trip surfaced three separate, real things worth Joseph's attention — which is the point of this section.
